children.value: . It is the quantum analog of a Taylor expansion: the key is finding the small parameter to expand in.
Expand the energies and eigenfunctions as power series in a bookkeeping parameter

children.html: λ\lambdaλ

children.value: , then collect terms order by order to get corrections of increasing order. In practice the first- and second-order corrections are usually enough for quantitatively accurate results.
Perturbation theory lets us write all corrections entirely in terms of the eigenfunctions and eigenvalues of the exactly solved problem, through matrix elements

children.html: Hnk=⟨n0∣H^1∣k0⟩H_{nk} = \langle n^0|\hat{H}^1|k^0\rangleHnk​=⟨n0∣H^1∣k0⟩

children.value: Hydrogen in a magnetic field, spin-orbit coupling, a shifted particle in a box, the anharmonic oscillator, and a perturbative picture of chemical bonding.
Perturbation theory attempts to solve analytically intractable problems by identifying an exactly

The method is similar in spirit to the Taylor expansion of continuous functions familiar from calculus. Just as in a Taylor expansion, the key is identifying the relatively small parameter in the problem to expand in.

Application of perturbation theory proceeds in two steps. Step one: identify the solvable part and the perturbation. Step two: expand the energy and eigenfunctions as a series of corrections of increasing order. In practice the first- and second-order corrections to the energy are sufficient to get quantitatively accurate results.
Perturbation theory allows us to write down expressions entirely in terms of the eigenfunctions and eigenvalues of the exactly solved problem.

Fig.1 Perturbation theory quantifies how much the energy levels shift when a small deviation is added to an exactly solvable Hamiltonian. For many problems that are impossible to solve exactly, one can still identify part of the Hamiltonian as exactly solvable,

children.html: H^0\hat{H}^0H^0

children.value: , with the rest treated as a perturbation.

children.value: the sum of the superscript indices determines
the order of the perturbation expansion.
zeroth order is just the exact solution.
Hamiltonian only has a first-order term
, while the eigenfunctions and eigenvalues are expanded to infinitely many terms. Usually going to second order is enough for most problems.

children.value: if the matrix elements are of comparable magnitude, neighboring energy levels make the larger contributions to the perturbation expression, because their small energy denominators amplify the term.
Derivations of the first- and second-order corrections

children.label: Derivations of the first- and second-order corrections

children.html: En1E^1_nEn1​

children.value: If the zeroth-order eigenfunctions are normalized, the unperturbed eigenfunction is orthogonal to all higher-order corrections:

children.html: ⟨n0∣n0⟩=1\langle n^0 \mid n^0 \rangle=1⟨n0∣n0⟩=1

children.value: \langle n^0\mid n\rangle = \langle n^0\mid n^0\rangle + \lambda\langle n^0\mid n^1\rangle=1+0+...
children.html: ⟨n0∣n⟩=⟨n0∣n0⟩+λ⟨n0∣n1⟩=1+0+...\langle n^0\mid n\rangle = \langle n^0\mid n^0\rangle + \lambda\langle n^0\mid n^1\rangle=1+0+...⟨n0∣n⟩=⟨n0∣n0⟩+λ⟨n0∣n1⟩=1+0+...
⟨n0∣nk⟩=0,k=1,2,…\langle n^0 \mid n^{k} \rangle=0, \quad k=1,2,\dots⟨n0∣nk⟩=0,k=1,2,…

children.value: Start from the first-order perturbation equation:

children.html: H^0∣n1⟩+H^1∣n0⟩=En0∣n1⟩+En1∣n0⟩{\hat{H}^0\mid n^1\rangle +\hat{H}^1\mid n^0\rangle = E^0_n\mid n^1 \rangle+E^1_n\mid n^0 \rangle}H^0∣n1⟩+H^1∣n0⟩=En0​∣n1⟩+En1​∣n0⟩
⟨n0∣\langle n^0 \mid⟨n0∣

children.value: and use the Hermitian property of the Hamiltonian:
{{\langle n^0} \mid \hat{H}^0\mid n^1\rangle +{\langle n^0} \mid  \hat{H}^1\mid n^0\rangle = E^0_n{\langle n^0} \mid n^1 \rangle+E^1_n {\langle n^0} \mid n^0 \rangle}
children.html: ⟨n0∣H^0∣n1⟩+⟨n0∣H^1∣n0⟩=En0⟨n0∣n1⟩+En1⟨n0∣n0⟩{{\langle n^0} \mid \hat{H}^0\mid n^1\rangle +{\langle n^0} \mid \hat{H}^1\mid n^0\rangle = E^0_n{\langle n^0} \mid n^1 \rangle+E^1_n {\langle n^0} \mid n^0 \rangle}⟨n0∣H^0∣n1⟩+⟨n0∣H^1∣n0⟩=En0​⟨n0∣n1⟩+En1​⟨n0∣n0⟩

children.value: The first terms on each side cancel by orthogonality, since

children.html: ⟨n0∣H^0∣n1⟩=En0⟨n0∣n1⟩=0\langle n^0 \mid \hat{H}^0\mid n^1\rangle = E^0_n \langle n^0 \mid n^1\rangle = 0⟨n0∣H^0∣n1⟩=En0​⟨n0∣n1⟩=0

children.value: We obtain the central result of perturbation theory, the first-order correction to the energy:

children.html: En1=⟨n0∣H^1∣n0⟩\color{red}{E_n^1 = \langle n^0 \mid \hat{H}^1\mid n^0 \rangle}En1​=⟨n0∣H^1∣n0⟩
En=En0+En1=En0+⟨n0∣H^1∣n0⟩\boxed{E_n=\color{green}{E^0_n}+\color{red}{E^1_n}=\color{green}{E^0_n}+\color{red}{\langle n^0 \mid \hat{H}^1\mid n^0 \rangle}}En​=En0​+En1​=En0​+⟨n0∣H^1∣n0⟩​

children.value: Example 1: Second-order correction to the ground state
Write the second-order correction explicitly for the ground state of some exactly solvable Hamiltonian

children.html: H^0\hat{H}^0H^0
H^1\hat{H}^1H^1
En=En0+Hnn+∑k≠n∣Hnk∣2En0−Ek0E_n = E^0_n+ H_{nn} + \sum_{k \neq n} \frac{\mid H_{nk}\mid^2}{E^0_n-E^0_k}En​=En0​+Hnn​+k=n∑​En0​−Ek0​∣Hnk​∣2​

E0=E00+H00+∣H01∣2E00−E10+∣H02∣2E00−E20+∣H03∣2E00−E30+...E_0 =E^0_0+ H_{00} + \frac{\mid H_{01}\mid^2}{E^0_0-E^0_1}+\frac{\mid H_{02}\mid^2}{E^0_0-E^0_2}+ \frac{\mid H_{03}\mid^2}{E^0_0-E^0_3}+ ...E0​=E00​+H00​+E00​−E10​∣H01​∣2​+E00​−E20​∣H02​∣2​+E00​−E30​∣H03​∣2​+...

children.value: Notice that for the ground state the second-order correction is always negative, because

children.html: ΔE0k=E0−Ek<0\Delta E_{0k}=E_0-E_k<0ΔE0k​=E0​−Ek​<0

children.value: Example 2: Magnetic field and spin-orbit coupling
A hydrogen atom in a magnetic field can be viewed as the H-atom Hamiltonian plus a small perturbation from the interaction with the field:

children.html: H^=H^0+e2meBL^z=H^0+H^1\hat{H}=\hat{H}_0 + \frac{e}{2m_e} B \hat{L}_z = \hat{H}_0 + \hat{H}^1H^=H^0​+2me​e​BL^z​=H^0​+H^1

children.value: Example 3: Perturbing the particle in a box
Estimate the ground-state and first-excited-state energies, within first-order perturbation theory, of a particle in a box with an added constant potential:

children.html: V(x)=V0,0≤x≤LV(x) = V_0, \quad 0 \leq x \leq LV(x)=V0​,0≤x≤L

children.value: The anharmonic oscillator is a harmonic oscillator plus a cubic perturbation:

children.html: H^=K^+kx22+γx3=H^0+γx3\hat{H} = \hat{K}+ \frac{kx^2}{2} +\gamma x^3 = \hat{H}_0+\gamma x^3H^=K^+2kx2​+γx3=H^0​+γx3

children.value: The first-order correction vanishes by symmetry, since

children.html: x3x^3x3
∣ψn∣2|\psi_n|^2∣ψn​∣2
En1=⟨n∣γx3∣n⟩=⟨even/odd∣odd∣even/odd⟩=0E_n^1 = \langle n\mid \gamma x^3 \mid n\rangle = \langle \text{even/odd} \mid \text{odd} \mid \text{even/odd}\rangle = 0En1​=⟨n∣γx3∣n⟩=⟨even/odd∣odd∣even/odd⟩=0

children.value: The energy levels must still change, so we turn to second order. For the ground state:

children.html: E02=∑k≠0∣⟨0∣γx3∣k⟩∣2E00−Ek0=∣⟨0∣γx3∣1⟩∣2E00−E10+∣⟨0∣γx3∣3⟩∣2E00−E30+...E^2_0 = \sum_{k \neq 0} \frac{\mid \langle 0\mid \gamma x^3 \mid k\rangle \mid^2}{E^0_0-E^0_k} = \frac{\mid \langle 0\mid \gamma x^3 \mid 1\rangle \mid^2}{E^0_0-E^0_1}+\frac{\mid \langle 0\mid \gamma x^3 \mid 3\rangle \mid^2}{E^0_0-E^0_3}+ ...E02​=k=0∑​E00​−Ek0​∣⟨0∣γx3∣k⟩∣2​=E00​−E10​∣⟨0∣γx3∣1⟩∣2​+E00​−E30​∣⟨0∣γx3∣3⟩∣2​+...
E0=ℏω2+0+H012ℏω+H0322ℏω+...E_0 = \frac{\hbar \omega}{2} + 0 + \frac{H^2_{01}}{\hbar \omega}+\frac{H^2_{03}}{2\hbar \omega}+ ...E0​=2ℏω​+0+ℏωH012​​+2ℏωH032​​+...

children.value: Only odd terms in the sum contribute. The matrix elements must be evaluated explicitly using Hermite polynomials.

Problem 2: Sign of the ground-state second-order shift
Argue, without evaluating any integrals, that the second-order energy correction to the ground state of any system is always negative or zero.

children.html: E0(2)=∑k≠0∣H0k∣2E00−Ek0E_0^{(2)} = \sum_{k\neq 0}\frac{|H_{0k}|^2}{E_0^0 - E_k^0}E0(2)​=∑k=0​E00​−Ek0​∣H0k​∣2​
∣H0k∣2≥0|H_{0k}|^2 \ge 0∣H0k​∣2≥0

children.value: for every term. Since the ground state is the lowest level,

children.html: E00−Ek0<0E_0^0 - E_k^0 < 0E00​−Ek0​<0
k≠0k \neq 0k=0

children.value: . Each term is therefore negative or zero, so the sum is negative or zero. Physically, mixing in excited states always pushes the ground state down.
